ideas.Material conditions shape consciousness:
"It is not the consciousness of men that determines their being, but, on the contrary, their social being that determines their consciousness." This principle — the core of **historical materialism** — is Marx's great inversion of the idealist tradition, and the foundation of his whole theory of history and society. Against Hegel, for whom history is the self-development of Spirit and ideas drive events, Marx insists that the *material* conditions of life — how human beings produce their means of subsistence — are primary, and that ideas, beliefs, laws, morality, religion, and philosophy arise *from* those conditions rather than the reverse.

Marx captures this in the architectural metaphor of **base and superstructure**. The economic "base" consists of the **forces of production** (technology, tools, labor) and the **relations of production** (who owns what, the class structure). Upon this base rises a "superstructure" of political institutions, legal systems, and forms of consciousness. When the base changes — as feudal agriculture gives way to industrial capitalism — the superstructure is transformed to match. And the prevailing ideas of an age, moreover, tend to be the ideas of its ruling class: **ideology** that presents the interests of the dominant class as natural, universal, and eternal.

This is not crude economic determinism; Marx and later Marxists granted the superstructure a "relative autonomy" and reciprocal influence. But the guiding claim is that to understand a society's thought, one must look to its mode of production and class relations. The principle grounds Marx's critique of religion (as the "opium of the people," reflecting real misery), his critique of ideology, and his conviction that changing the world requires changing its material foundations, not merely its ideas.
ideas.Class struggle as motor of history:
"The history of all hitherto existing society is the history of class struggles." With this opening line of the *Communist Manifesto*, Marx and Engels announce their theory of historical change. History does not advance through the play of great ideas, national spirits, or accidents, but through the **conflict between classes** defined by their relation to the means of production — freeman and slave, patrician and plebeian, lord and serf, and now, in the modern epoch, **bourgeoisie** (the owners of capital) and **proletariat** (the wage-laborers who own only their labor power).

The mechanism is driven by the tension between the developing forces of production and the existing relations of production. As productive powers grow, they come into conflict with the property relations and class structure of the established order; this contradiction sharpens into class struggle, and eventually the old order is overthrown and a new mode of production, with a new dominant class, takes its place. Feudalism thus gave way to capitalism as the rising bourgeoisie burst the "fetters" of the feudal order.

Capitalism, Marx argues, is generating its own gravediggers. By concentrating workers in factories and cities, immiserating them, and stripping away comforting illusions, it forges the proletariat into a class capable of revolutionary consciousness and collective action. The class struggle between bourgeoisie and proletariat will, Marx predicts, culminate in a proletarian revolution that abolishes private property in the means of production and, with it, class division itself — ending the "prehistory" of humanity and inaugurating a classless communist society. Whether history in fact moves this way became one of the great questions of the twentieth century.
ideas.Alienated labor under capitalism:
Alienation (*Entfremdung*) is the central concept of the young Marx's *Economic and Philosophic Manuscripts of 1844*, and his most humanistic critique of capitalism. For Marx, human beings realize their nature through **productive labor** — through freely and creatively transforming the world and objectifying themselves in what they make. Under capitalism, however, labor becomes its opposite: a source not of self-realization but of self-loss. The worker, selling his labor power for a wage, is **alienated** in four interlocking ways.

First, from the **product** of his labor: what he makes belongs to the capitalist, confronts him as an alien object, and the more he produces the poorer and more dominated he becomes. Second, from the **act of production**: work is not the free expression of his powers but forced, monotonous, externally controlled drudgery, in which he "does not affirm himself but denies himself," feeling at home only outside of work. Third, from his **species-being** (*Gattungswesen*) — his distinctively human nature as a free, conscious, creative producer — which is reduced to a mere means to physical survival. And fourth, from **other people**: relations between workers, and between worker and capitalist, become competitive and instrumental.

Alienation names the deep human cost of a system in which labor, the very activity that should fulfill us, is turned against us and appropriated by another. The concept ties Marx's economics to a vision of human flourishing: the point of overcoming capitalism is not merely fairer distribution but the recovery of unalienated, freely creative human activity. Rediscovered in the twentieth century, the young Marx's theory of alienation profoundly shaped Western Marxism, existentialism, and the Frankfurt School.
ideas.Critique of political economy:
"A Critique of Political Economy" is the subtitle of *Capital*, and it names Marx's mature life's work: the rigorous, systematic analysis of how capitalism actually functions, undertaken in order to expose its inner laws and contradictions. Marx took the "classical political economy" of Adam Smith and David Ricardo with immense seriousness — he adopted its **labor theory of value**, that the value of a commodity is determined by the socially necessary labor time required to produce it — but he turned its own tools against the system it had largely celebrated.

His central discovery is **surplus value**, the source of profit and the secret of exploitation. The capitalist buys the worker's *labor power* at its value (roughly, the cost of the worker's subsistence), but sets it to work for a full day. That labor power produces *more* value than it costs — the worker reproduces his wages in part of the day and works the rest, in effect, for free. This unpaid surplus, appropriated by the capitalist, is the origin of profit. Exploitation is thus built into the wage relation itself, and hidden beneath the appearance of a free and equal exchange.

Marx also analyzed the "**fetishism of commodities**" — the way social relations between people take on the illusory form of relations between things, so that the market appears as a natural force rather than a human product. And he charted capitalism's internal contradictions: the recurring crises of overproduction, the tendency of the rate of profit to fall, and the concentration of capital, which he believed would drive the system toward breakdown. Whatever one makes of its predictions, it remains the most powerful critical anatomy of capitalism ever written.

works.Capital:
*Capital: A Critique of Political Economy* (*Das Kapital*) is Marx's monumental masterwork — the mature, systematic analysis of the capitalist mode of production to which he devoted the last decades of his life. Only the first volume (1867) appeared before his death; Engels edited the second and third from Marx's manuscripts. Dense, difficult, and encyclopedic, it aims to lay bare "the economic law of motion of modern society."

Volume I begins, famously, not with grand history but with the **commodity** — the "cell-form" of capitalist wealth — and patiently builds outward from it. Marx develops the **labor theory of value**, distinguishes use-value from exchange-value, and unveils the "**fetishism of commodities**," whereby human social relations appear as relations between things. He then reveals the secret of profit in **surplus value**: the capitalist extracts unpaid labor from the worker, so that exploitation is structural, concealed within the apparently fair wage contract. He analyzes the working day, machinery, and the relentless drive to accumulate, and closes with a searing historical account of "primitive accumulation" — the violent dispossession that created the propertyless working class.

The later volumes examine the circulation of capital and the distribution of surplus value as profit, interest, and rent, and develop the controversial law of the tendency of the rate of profit to fall. Combining economic analysis, philosophy, history, and blistering moral indictment, *Capital* is at once a work of science and a call to revolution. Whatever the fate of its specific predictions, it remains the single most influential critique of capitalism ever written, and the foundation of the tradition that bears Marx's name.
works.The German Ideology:
*The German Ideology*, written by Marx and Engels in 1845–46 but left unpublished in their lifetimes (famously "abandoned to the gnawing criticism of the mice"), is the work in which **historical materialism** receives its first full statement. Settling accounts with the Young Hegelians — Feuerbach, Bruno Bauer, and Max Stirner — who thought that changing *consciousness* could liberate humanity, Marx and Engels lay out a rival, materialist conception of history.

Their thesis is that the real premises of history are not ideas but "real individuals, their activity, and the material conditions under which they live." Human beings are distinguished from animals by the fact that they *produce* their means of subsistence, and *how* they produce — the **mode of production** — shapes everything else: social organization, class relations, and forms of consciousness. "Life is not determined by consciousness, but consciousness by life." **Ideology** — including philosophy, morality, and religion — is a reflection of material life, and the ruling ideas of any age are the ideas of its ruling class.

The book sketches the succession of modes of production through history and gives an early, evocative vision of communism as a society that abolishes the fixed division of labor, so that one might "hunt in the morning, fish in the afternoon, rear cattle in the evening, criticize after dinner." Though a rough, polemical, and unfinished manuscript, *The German Ideology* is indispensable: it is where Marx breaks decisively with Hegelian idealism and Feuerbachian humanism and founds the materialist theory of history that underlies all his later work.
works.The Communist Manifesto:
*The Communist Manifesto* (1848), written by Marx and Engels as the program of the Communist League, is the most influential political pamphlet ever written — a short, electrifying document that compressed their theory of history and their revolutionary call into a few incandescent pages. Composed on the eve of the revolutions that swept Europe in 1848, it opens with the famous image of a "spectre haunting Europe — the spectre of communism."

Its first section presents the theory of history in miniature: "The history of all hitherto existing society is the history of **class struggles**." It offers a strikingly prescient and even admiring portrait of the **bourgeoisie** as a revolutionary force that has torn away feudal illusions, globalized production, and constantly transformed the world — "all that is solid melts into air" — while also creating, in the **proletariat**, the class destined to overthrow it. The second section sets out the aims of the communists, above all the abolition of private property in the means of production, and answers bourgeois objections. Later sections survey rival socialisms and revolutionary tactics.

It closes with two of the most famous lines in political literature: that the workers "have nothing to lose but their chains" and "a world to win," and the ringing call, "**Working men of all countries, unite!**" Combining historical analysis, rhetorical fire, and a concrete program, the *Manifesto* became the founding text of the international communist movement and one of the most consequential documents of the modern age — read, translated, memorized, and fought over across the entire world.
